flashcards.front: What is thrashing in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Thrashing is a severe performance degradation phenomenon where the system spends more time swapping pages in and out (handling page faults) than executing actual processes, resulting in very low CPU utilization.

flashcards.front: What is the primary cause of thrashing?
flashcards.back: Thrashing occurs when there are not enough frames in memory to hold the working set of active processes, causing excessive page faults that lead to constant page swapping.

flashcards.front: What is the working set model?
flashcards.back: The working set model defines the set of pages a process is actively using. If the working set size exceeds the allocated frames, the process will thrash as it constantly needs to bring pages into memory.

flashcards.front: What is page fault frequency (PFF) control?
flashcards.back: A thrashing control technique that monitors the page fault rate of each process. If the rate is too high, more frames are allocated; if too low, frames are taken away to improve multiprogramming level.

flashcards.front: How does thrashing affect CPU utilization?
flashcards.back: As thrashing increases, CPU utilization decreases significantly because the CPU is idle waiting for I/O operations (page swaps) to complete rather than executing user processes.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between degree of multiprogramming and thrashing?
flashcards.back: When too many processes are loaded in memory (high degree of multiprogramming), each gets fewer frames, leading to more page faults and eventually thrashing. There's an optimal point that maximizes CPU utilization.
In local replacement, a process can only replace pages from its own allocated frames. This prevents one process from affecting another's page fault rate but doesn't resolve thrashing fairly.
In global replacement, any process can replace pages from the entire pool of free frames. This is more flexible but can allow one thrashing process to degrade system-wide performance.
A thrashing control technique where one or more processes are completely swapped out to disk (suspended) to free up all their frames for active processes, reducing thrashing.
Prepaging brings pages into memory before they are actually needed, based on predictions. This reduces page faults when a suspended process resumes execution.

flashcards.front: What is the page fault frequency curve?
flashcards.back: A graph showing CPU utilization versus degree of multiprogramming. It rises initially, peaks at optimal multiprogramming, then falls sharply during thrashing.

flashcards.front: How does increasing frame allocation affect a thrashing process?
flashcards.back: Increasing the number of frames allocated to a thrashing process gives it more memory for its working set, reducing page faults and allowing it to complete execution faster.
Virtual memory thrashing occurs when the demand for pages exceeds the physical memory capacity, causing the system to constantly swap pages between disk and memory.

Thrashing can be prevented by: 1) Using working set model, 2) Page fault frequency control, 3) Process suspension, 4) Proper frame allocation, 5) Keeping the degree of multiprogramming at optimal level.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between hard thrashing and soft thrashing?
flashcards.back: Hard thrashing occurs when a process cannot make progress due to constant page faults. Soft thrashing is when performance degrades but the process still makes some progress.
